b. l,{ay the committee use a tele-network to coLlect the
rnoney raised?
CONCLUS IONS
1. A canpaign nay raise funds bY use
telephone Iine, provided the procedures for
reporting of contributions cornply lrith the
of a 9 oo-number
solicitation 
and
provisions of the Act.
2. The television advertisement and the message provided
over the 9oo-number line should contain a statement that the call
will result in a contribution to the candidate.
3. The Act does not prohibit the rnaking of a contrj.bution by
credit card. It is also perrnissible to collect the contribution
by neans of a tele-network using the telephone conPany as a col-
lection agent. However, any costs associated with the collection
is an expenditure of the conmittee. The full amount charged to
the caller is the amount of the contribution.
EACTS
You are requesting advice on behalf of the Lungren ca:npaign
com:nittee regarding the raising of canpaign funds by use of a 900
telephone number. The committee would run a televisj.on commercial
at the end of rrhich would appear a message inviting the viewer to
call a 9oo number for further infornation concerning the Lungren
campaign. The message reould also notify the viewer that the 900
call is a toll ca1l.
A person lrho caIls the 900 nunber will be presented trith
several options which nay result in direct or indirect contribu-
tions to the candidate. The connittee does not foresee problens
keeping track of the contributions. 
You have raised questj.ons
concerning issues which may arise under the Act under each option.
option A
Under option A, the caller will hear a message thanking hin
or her for calling and askj.ng if the call,er wishes to hear the
candidate,s position on various issues. If the caller chooses to
hear a nessage, he or she selects the nunber of the message and
receives that recorded nessage. The caller will be charged for


our File No. I-9 o-2 3 9
Page 3
the first minute and for every minute thereafter, beginning in-
nediately after the call is ansrrered. The telephone company $/ould
receive a portion of that charge as a user fee. The remaj-ning
noney would be recej.ved by the committee.
option B
option B would include the process set forth in Option A, but
rroul.d also ask the caller if he or she vrould li.ke to make a
contribution to the Lungren carnpaign. The caller e/ould then press
a number for contributions and make a contribution to the
campaign.
ANALYS I S
We have previously rendered written advice on the issue of
telephone fundraj.sing to soticit credit card contributions.
(Schwartz Advice Letter No. A-78-193, copy enclosed.) In
schwartz, lre !/ere asked j.f the Act prohibj.ted fundraising activity
in which donors would charge political contributions to their
credit card accounts by telephone. The contributor would make the
telephone contribution by calling a toll-free 
number shown on
television during a political commercial. The cal-Ier t ould be
connected with the staff of a toll-free answering service who
would record the amount of the contribution and the caller's
credit card number. This inforrnation would be forwarded to the
candidate's bank to be credited to the candidate's account in the
anount of the contribution. 
The credit card conpany would then
bill the caller.
The advice provided in Sch$/artz was that the Act would not
prohibit such fundraising, but that the operation would have to
comply with the requirements of the Act. The advice provided in
Schwartz was rendered in 1978. Therefore, we vill review the
requirements imposed by the Act as amended and pursuant to regula-
tions interpreting the Act which nay have been adopted in the
interin.
I. 
Section 84300(c) provides that any contribution of $L00
or more must be nade by written instrurnent containing the nane of
the donor and the naDe of the payee. Thus, for any contribution
of $1oo or more during a calendar year, a copy of the credj-t card
voucher must be sent to the connittee receiving the contribution.
The voucher must be sent as soon as practicable after the donor
instructs that the contribution be nade. (Sch$rartz, supra. )
2. Section 84104, Regulation 18401, and the Conrnission's
recordkeepinq guidelines provide that for each contribution of S25
or more from a single source, the following information must be
retained by a candidate or coruarj.ttee: the contributor,s full
name, street address, occupation, employer or name of business if
self-enpl,oyed, date and anount of each contribution received
during the reporting period and, if a loan, the interest rate, and
the cumulative arnount of contributions. section 84211(f) requires


Our File No. I-9 o-2 3 9
Page 4
that the foregoing information be itenized on the campaign
statenent for any person whose cumuLative contributions are SI,OO
or more during a calendar year.
3. sectj,on 84302 requires that anyone acting as an
internediary or agent of another in naking a contribution of glOO
or more disclose hj-s or her own name, street address, occupation,
enployer or, if self-enployed, his or her principal place of
business and also disclose the sane information as to the
contributor. 
Information concerning both the internediary and the
contributor nust be disclosed on the commj.ttee's campaign
statenent. A person is an internediary if he or she delivers a
contrj.bution to the committee fron another person unless the
contribution is from the person's employer, irunediate farnj-1y or an
association to which the person belongs. The treasurer of the
commj.ttee and the controlling candidate are not j.nternediar j.es .
(section 84302.5.) This infornatj.on would have to be obtained
from the caller and provided to the conmittee.
It should be noted that an interrnediary does not include a
person who rris a canpaign consultant, professional fundraiser, or
other independent contractor, e/ho is under contract to provide
canpaign services to the candidate or cornrnittee to which the
contribution is made, if acting within the scope of his or her
contract.'r (Regulation 18432.5, copy enclosed.)
4. Section 84306 requires that aII contributions received by
a person acting as an agent of a candidate or conmittee be
reported pronptly to the candidate or committee treasurer.
rrProtnptlyrr neans no later than the closing date of any carnpaign
statenent the candidate or co:nmittee is required to fiIe.
5. Sections 85301 and 85303 itrrpose limits upon contributions
by persons or connittees to a candidate or the candidate,s
controlled conmittee and also prohibit a candidate or campaign
treasurer frorn sol. j.citing contributions in excess of those linits.
6. Section 85201(e) provides that al.I carnpaigrn expenditures
sha1l be made frorn the canpaign bank account. Since there nay be
a delay in receipt of contributions bilted, it is possible that
sone expenditures will be incurred prior to receipt of the
contributions. In order to assure that all expendj-tures are made
from the carnpaign account, the estiurated pre-contribution costs
should be paid by the canpaign as a deposit. Alternatively,
Regrulation 18526 (copy enclosed) provides that expenditures paid
by an agent or j.ndependent contractor will be deemed expenditures
made from the campaign bank account if certain requirernents are
net.
(1) The expenditures are nade pursuant to a writ-
ten contract between the candidate or conrnittee and the
agent or independent contractor which provides for the
reimbursement of such expend j,ture.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-239
Page 5
(2) The treasurer of the committee is provided
with a dated receipt and a written description of each
expenditure prior to reimbursement.
(3) Reimbursement is paid within 45 calendar days
after the expenditures are paid by the person to be
reinbursed .
Regulation 18526(b) (1) - (3).
If reimbursement is not paid within 45 calendar days, the
expenditure must be reported as a nonnonetary contribution
received on the 45th day after the expenditure was paid by the
agent or independent contractor, unless that person has nade a
good faith effort to obtaj.n reimbursement and has been unable to
collect. 
(Regulation 18526 (d) . )
Contribution bv credit card.
So long as the above requirements are net, the Act does not
prohibit the rnaking of a contri,bution by credit card. The amount
charged on the credit card is the amount of the contribution which
must be reported. Any fees paid to the credit card company for
this service will be reported as an expenditure on Schedule E of
the canpaiqn statement.
contribution placed on ca11er's telephone bi11.
If the contribution is placed upon the ca11er's telephone
bill, 
a question is raised as to when the contribution is rrmade.rl
For purposes of thj,s analysis, i.t is assuned that the telephone
company will not forward the I'contributionrr to the conmittee until
the cal1er pays the telephone bil1. 
unless the telephone company
has a contractual right to enforce the pa)rnent of such a charge on
the telephone biII, 
pJ.acing the contribution on the telephone bill
is not a contrj.bution. Holrever, vhen the payment is received by
the telephone company a contribution has been nade. As indicated
above, and in Schwartz, supra, any telePhone fundraising operation
must cornply rrith the Act. Therefore, if collection is to occur
through the telephone cornpany, the telephone conpany will have to
collect and provide to the carnpaign in a tinely fashion all
information necessary for canpaign reporting, including the date
of the contribution and required information about the
contribution.
Contribution collected bv tele-netr{rork.
you indicated in our telephone conversation of April 23,
1990, that collection by rrtele-networkrr refers to a systen by
which a tele-network organization leases goo-number lines fron the
telephone conpany. The organization contracts with the canpaign
to put a recorded nessage on the organization's lines. 
callers
are billed by the telephone company for each goo-nunber cal1. 
The
telephone conpany merely acts as the billing agent for the


Our File No. I-9 O-2 3 9
Page 6
organization, and forwards the amounts collected after deducting
its costs. In accordance with its contract with the carnpaign, the
organization then deducts its fee and remits the baLance to the
carnpaign.
Subject to the same requirements of the Act as set forth
above, the tele-network systern of collection is not prohibited by
the Act. As in the case of the credit card contribution, the
amount of the contribution is the anount collected from the
caller. 
Any portions retained by the tele-netrrork are treated as
fundraising costs by the canpaign.
Contribution of ttprof itrr under option A.
The procedure set forth in Option A differs somewhat from
that in Sch$rartz, because the caller does not expressly nake a
contribution to the campaign. Therefore, a statement should be
included advising the caller that the cost of the call is a
contribution to the conmittee. Thi,s service is in the nature of a
fundraising event to which the cal1er gains access by nakinq the
toII caIl. 
The cost of the telephone call paid by the caller is a
contribution fron the catler. 
The anounts retained by the
telephone company and the tele-network organization are costs of
the fundraiser.
I trust that this letter has provided you with the quidance
that you need. If you have any questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-seo1.
SincereLy, -
scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel

counsel, Legal Division
